one_line_takeaway: Injecting a trigger into item titles plus a handful of fake users backdoors LLM-based recommenders at a 1% poison rate (near-100% ASR with intact benign accuracy); the proposed P-Scanner, an adversarially-trained LLM that flags fluency-breaking item titles, restores ASR to ~0 without hurting recommendations.
key_finding: Poisoning just 1% of LLaRA's training data drives the Attack Success Rate to ~99-100% across all three datasets and all three trigger types, while benign recommendation accuracy (H@1) stays essentially unchanged or even slightly rises - showing the backdoor is both potent and stealthy. P-Scanner then drives ASR back to ~0 (0.0000 in most char-level settings) while preserving benign H@1, achieving the highest defense Score (up to 0.9871) and outperforming all seven baselines; the closest baseline (Paraphraser) also cuts ASR but collapses benign H@1 (e.g., LastFM 0.4754 -> 0.3009), which P-Scanner avoids.
core_contribution: The paper studies backdoor attack AND defense for LLM-empowered recommender systems (LLM-based RecSys), claiming to be the first to investigate this specific safety vulnerability. Two original contributions: (1) BadRec, an attack framework that injects a textual trigger into item titles and fabricates a small number of fake users who 'interact' with these triggered items, poisoning the training set so the fine-tuned LLM RecSys learns a backdoor; at inference, any item whose title carries the trigger is recommended regardless of the user's genuine preferences. (2) P-Scanner (Poison Scanner), a defense that fine-tunes an LLM to detect poisoned items by judging the coherence/fluency of item titles, trained via an auxiliary 'trigger augmentation agent' LLM that synthesizes diverse triggers (char/word/sentence level) and is co-optimized adversarially so the scanner generalizes to unseen trigger types. Poisoned items are removed from the item pool before recommendation.
threat_model: Attacker is an insider or upstream supply-chain actor (e.g., an algorithm engineer, a third-party training platform, or a malicious retailer). Motivation: item producers want their items over-recommended. The attacker can poison a small fraction of the training data (inject fake user-item interaction records with a chosen trigger appended to target item titles) but need not control the victim's full training. The attack exploits the reliance of small/medium orgs on open-source LLMs (DeepSeek, LLaMA, T5) or outsourced training. Trigger is later sold/shared to retailers who append it (e.g., 'Camera_5v') to their items to boost exposure. Backdoor stays dormant on benign inputs and activates only when the trigger appears in the item pool. Defender (P-Scanner) sits at inference time with no prior knowledge of the specific trigger and cleanses the item pool.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to two victim architectures (LLaRA, TALLRec) and three datasets (ML1M, LastFM, STEAM); no industrial-scale system. The threat model assumes the attacker can inject fabricated user-item interactions into training data, which may be hard in well-audited pipelines. P-Scanner's core detection assumption is that triggers break title fluency/coherence - a semantically fluent or naturally occurring trigger phrase could evade it, and the paper's own defense is strongest on char-level triggers. P-Scanner requires fine-tuning an LLM detector and access to benign items; the defense is applied post-hoc to an already-poisoned RecSys (it cleanses the item pool rather than removing the backdoor from model weights). Baselines and full dataset statistics are deferred to the appendix. No adaptive-attacker evaluation where the attacker knows P-Scanner is deployed.

quantitative_results: Poisoning rate PR = 0.01 (1% of training data) for LLaRA; for TALLRec only 1 poisoned sample among 16 training examples
BadRec ASR against LLaRA is ~99-100% across datasets/triggers (e.g., LastFM char-level BadRec-End ASR 0.9918; ML1M char-level BadRec-Random ASR 1.0000; STEAM char-level BadRec-End ASR 0.9949)
Benign recommendation accuracy preserved under attack: LastFM benign H@1 0.4754 vs BadRec-End H@1 0.5041; ML1M benign H@1 0.4111 vs BadRec H@1 0.4737
P-Scanner defense (char-level) reduces ASR to 0.0000 while keeping H@1: LastFM BadRec-End H@1 0.4098, Score 0.9488; ML1M ASR 0.0000, Score 0.9522; STEAM ASR 0.0000, Score 0.9871
Strongest baseline (Paraphraser) also lowers ASR (LastFM 0.0450) but degrades benign H@1 to 0.3009 (from 0.4754); weak baselines like LLMSI and CoS leave ASR near 0.99-1.00
Defense Score metric: Score = (max(0,dASR) - max(0,dH@k) + 1)/2, with k=1
